metadata.subject: English Language Arts
metadata.description: Composition 10 develops students' skills in written communication through engagement with a variety of forms, genres, and purposes. Students learn to write with clarity, purpose, and voice — crafting both literary and informational texts for real audiences. The course emphasizes the writing process, revision, and developing a writer's voice, while also building reading skills that inform and improve writing.
metadata.general_description: A focused study of composition and writing craft, developing skills in planning, drafting, revising, and publishing literary and informational texts for diverse purposes and audiences.
metadata.grade_10_module_note: Composition 10 is one of the modular Grade 10 ELA courses. It is commonly paired with Literary Studies 10.

big_ideas.statement: The exploration of text and story deepens our understanding of diverse, complex ideas about identity, others, and the world.
big_ideas.elaboration: Reading widely fuels better writing — exposure to diverse texts enriches a writer's sense of what language can do.

big_ideas.statement: Texts are socially, culturally, geographically, and historically constructed.
big_ideas.elaboration: Every piece of writing — including our own — is shaped by the context in which it is created, the audience it addresses, and the cultural assumptions of the writer.

big_ideas.statement: Language shapes ideas and influences others.
big_ideas.elaboration: Skilled writers understand that word choice, sentence structure, and form are not neutral — they shape how readers think and feel.

big_ideas.statement: Engagement with writing processes can support creativity and enhance clarity of expression.
big_ideas.elaboration: Writing is a craft that improves through deliberate practice, revision, and reflection. The writing process — not just the product — is a site of learning and growth.

categories.name: Comprehend and Connect (Reading, Listening, Viewing)
categories.raw: Comprehend and Connect — Reading, Listening, Viewing

competencies.raw: Recognize and appreciate the role of story in expressing First Peoples perspectives; recognize diversity within First Peoples societies.
i_can_statements: I can explain how First Peoples oral and written traditions can inform my own writing practice.
I can recognize the diversity of First Peoples voices in Canadian literature.
I can engage with First Peoples texts with respect for their cultural context and ownership protocols.

competencies.raw: Access information from varied sources; explore relevance, accuracy, and reliability to inform writing.
i_can_statements: I can research a topic using a variety of sources to generate ideas and evidence for my writing.
I can evaluate sources for credibility and relevance to my writing purpose.
I can take useful notes from reading that I can draw on in my own writing.

competencies.raw: Think critically and creatively about ideas within and beyond texts; identify bias, contradictions, and distortions.
i_can_statements: I can read mentor texts critically to understand how authors make intentional craft decisions.
I can identify bias or distortion in source material and consider how it might affect my writing.
I can use critical reading to generate ideas and questions that drive my own writing.

competencies.raw: Explore how language constructs personal and cultural identities; construct meaningful personal connections.
i_can_statements: I can analyze how a writer's word choices reveal their identity and values.
I can connect a text's themes to my own experiences and use those connections in my writing.
I can examine how my own cultural background and experiences shape my writing voice.

categories.name: Create and Communicate (Writing, Speaking, Representing)
categories.raw: Create and Communicate — Writing, Speaking, Representing

competencies.raw: Exchange ideas respectfully from diverse perspectives to build shared understanding.
i_can_statements: I can participate in writing workshops where I share drafts and give and receive feedback.
I can listen respectfully to different perspectives during peer feedback.
I can use collaborative discussion to generate ideas for my writing.

competencies.raw: Respond to text in personal, creative, and critical ways.
i_can_statements: I can write a response to a text that demonstrates both personal engagement and critical thinking.
I can use a text as a springboard for my own original writing.
I can explain how reading a particular text influenced my own writing choices.

competencies.raw: Refine texts to improve clarity and impact; demonstrate speaking and listening skills.
i_can_statements: I can read my writing aloud to evaluate rhythm, clarity, and impact.
I can revise my writing using specific criteria for clarity, voice, and structure.
I can present a piece of writing to an audience with appropriate delivery.

competencies.raw: Use writing and design processes to plan, develop, and create engaging texts for varied purposes and audiences.
i_can_statements: I can move through the full writing process: prewriting, drafting, revising, editing, and publishing.
I can make intentional choices about form, genre, and structure to suit my purpose and audience.
I can write in both literary and informational modes with skill and clarity.

competencies.raw: Express and support opinions with evidence; use Canadian conventions; use citations.
i_can_statements: I can write a clear thesis or main argument and support it with well-chosen evidence.
I can apply Canadian spelling, grammar, and punctuation conventions accurately.
I can properly cite and acknowledge sources using an accepted citation format.

competencies.raw: Transform ideas and information to create original texts.
i_can_statements: I can create original texts that draw on research and reading but reflect my own thinking and voice.
I can transform an idea from one form (e.g., research notes) into another (e.g., an essay or story).
I can demonstrate a distinctive writing voice that is authentically my own.

areas.name: Text Forms, Features, and Genres
areas.raw: Literary and thematic categories with narrative structures; narrative structures in First Peoples texts; protocols related to ownership of First Peoples oral texts.
topics: Literary forms: short story, personal essay, creative non-fiction, poetry, script
Informational forms: report, analytical essay, persuasive essay, argument
Text features: layout, headings, visuals, citations
Narrative structures: linear, non-linear, cyclical, iterative
First Peoples narrative structures and protocols

areas.name: Strategies and Processes
areas.raw: Reading, oral language, and metacognitive strategies; writing processes (determining audience/purpose, generating ideas, drafting, revising, editing).
topics: Drafting: getting ideas onto the page without over-editing
Revising: reconsidering structure, argument, clarity, and impact
Editing: grammar, punctuation, spelling, word choice
Publishing: sharing writing with authentic audiences
Metacognitive reflection on writing strengths and challenges

areas.name: Language Features, Structures, and Conventions
areas.raw: Elements of style; voice; usage and conventions; literary elements and devices; citation techniques.
topics: Elements of style: diction, sentence variety, tone, mood
Voice: developing an authentic writer's voice; point of view choices
Usage: avoiding common errors; applying grammar conventions
Literary and rhetorical devices: used purposefully in writing
Citation techniques: academic and creative formats
